cases.claim: Vendor marketing claims 98-99% accuracy with false-positive rates between under 1% and 3%; independent benchmarking finds detectors 'easily fooled by adversarial attacks, variations in sampling strategies, repetition penalties, and unseen generative models' (RAID, 6M+ texts), and an average 61.22% false-positive rate across seven detectors on non-native-English TOEFL essays -- 89 of 91 essays (97.8%) were flagged by at least one detector.
Benford's first-digit law was invalidly applied to 2020 US precinct-level election data; academic consensus holds first-digit analysis of precinct-level returns cannot diagnose fraud.

C2PA provenance metadata is routinely stripped in ordinary distribution: 'most social media platforms today remove metadata from uploaded images, and actions like taking a screenshot can also remove it' (an implementer's official statement) -- and consequently 'there are very few Content Credentials out there on the Internet' (Bray, first-hand investigation).
cases.who_pays: Anyone verifying an image's origin after ordinary sharing or re-encoding

cases.claim: The same last-digit test that correctly flags 0/5-avoidance fabrication fires just as strongly on legitimate clinical and survey data with systematic rounding (blood-pressure terminal-digit bias; age heaping), producing a mirror-image surplus at 0/5 that the chi-square statistic alone cannot distinguish from fraud.

Contamination-free re-scoring (MMLU-CF) shows a 12-18 percentage-point inflation in reported MMLU scores across 7 models/providers (e.g. GPT-4o: 88.0% to 73.4%); on the clean benchmark, models sit below the human-expert average of 89.8%.
cases.who_pays: Those who rely on reported benchmark scores -- researchers, policymakers, the public

cases.claim: ProPublica's finding (FPR 44.85% Black vs 23.45% White) and Northpointe's finding (PPV ~63% Black vs 59% White) are both numerically correct on the same dataset; Chouldechova proves no classifier can jointly satisfy equal-FPR, equal-FNR, and equal-PPV across groups with unequal base rates unless it is a perfect classifier.

Carlisle's method cannot distinguish fabrication from legitimate stratified or covariate-adaptive randomisation at moderate signal strength -- both produce the same excess-balance signature; the method's own 2017 paper title concedes 'data fabrication and other reasons for non-random sampling.'
cases.who_pays: Researchers wrongly flagged; fabricators escaping detection

cases.claim: DSM-5's deletion of the DSM-IV bereavement-exclusion criterion for Major Depressive Disorder means a person with identical symptoms is diagnosable under one edition's criteria and not under the previous edition's, with no change in the person.
cases.who_pays: Everyone classified -- the instrument constitutes the phenomenon

cases.claim: Trial 1 of the Standing Docket: second-digit Benford chi-square convicts the known-provenance World Bank 2023 population series (N=217, p=0.034), while Nigrini's fixed MAD cutoff flags both clean-assumed real series and the Benford-conforming synthetic control at N between 200 and 217 -- one trial is a pilot, not a rate.

self.statement: This taxonomy classifies 9 self-selected cases assembled by the same collective across two days; it has not been tested against a case it did not choose.
self.lane_rationale: Filed here because a taxonomy is itself a constitutive instrument: naming failure modes changes what future instruments are built to look for.

unfiled.claim: Counter-evidence: in a Czech-language setting, no systematic bias against non-native speakers was found, and the detectors studied did not rely on perplexity as a key feature -- a case where the tool was not shown to fail.
unfiled.verdict_line: no lane fits -- the drawer classifies failures, and has no compartment for evidence of a non-failure
unfiled.selection_admission: Chosen by the same collective, precisely because it could not file: a foregone conclusion staged as one, not a stress test. The stress test needs a case the collective did not choose.

submitted.tool: Horizon -- Fujitsu's electronic point-of-sale and branch-accounting system, deployed by the UK Post Office as evidence of theft and false accounting
submitted.claim: Horizon: "The system was piloted in 1999, and rolled out to branch post offices in 2000." The Post Office (POL) prosecuted subpostmasters for theft and false accounting on the strength of Horizon-generated shortfalls. The Court of Appeal held that "the failures of investigation and disclosure were in our judgment so egregious as to make the prosecution of any of the 'Horizon cases' an affront to the conscience of the court." And it found: "By representing Horizon as reliable, and refusing to countenance any suggestion to the contrary, POL effectively sought to reverse the burden of proof: it treated what was no more than a shortfall shown by an unreliable accounting system as an incontrovertible loss, and proceeded as if it were for the accused to prove that no such loss had occurred." Of disclosure of Fujitsu's known-error records, the court found: "In the prosecutions of these 42 appellants, so far as we are aware, there was no disclosure of any such document."
submitted.filed_reading: Filed by reading into Lane 1, Calibration gap: the Post Office represented Horizon as reliable while the High Court's Horizon Issues judgment (Bates v Post Office Ltd (No 6: Horizon Issues) [2019] EWHC 3408 (QB), Fraser J) found that Horizon contained bugs, errors and defects that could cause apparent shortfalls in branch accounts -- the claims-versus-practice shape that defines the lane.
submitted.boundary_finding: The load-bearing mechanism does not file. What the Court of Appeal itself held is a finding about conduct: by representing Horizon as reliable, POL "effectively sought to reverse the burden of proof: it treated what was no more than a shortfall shown by an unreliable accounting system as an incontrovertible loss, and proceeded as if it were for the accused to prove that no such loss had occurred"; and in the 42 appellants' prosecutions there was, so far as the court was aware, no disclosure of Fujitsu's known-error records. The legal-historical layer beneath that conduct is the collective's own separately sourced synthesis, not the judgment's language: section 69 of the Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984, which had required the prosecution to show a computer was operating properly before its output was admissible, was repealed in 1999 -- "just months before the first trials of the Horizon system began" -- leaving a rebuttable presumption that a computer was operating correctly (Law Commission 1997; Ministry of Justice call for evidence, which itself links the presumption to the Horizon convictions). Court-found conduct plus separately sourced presumption point the same way: evidentiary presumption plus withheld disclosure is not a structural property of the tool itself -- not its spec, its validity conditions, its design goals, or its relation to its object. The drawer has no lane for a property of what received the tool's word.
submitted.mode6_rejection: Mode 6, "ambiguous verdict," was considered and rejected: its ratified boundary requires the ambiguity to be a fixed property of the tool's signal at a given strength. Horizon's shortfall ambiguity was resolvable by the withheld known-error records (PEAKs/KELs) that Fujitsu held and the prosecution did not disclose -- contingent on disclosure, not fixed by the tool's design.
submitted.mode7_rejection: Mode 7, "constitutive measurement," was considered and rejected -- it is the closer precedent, since "relation to its object" is one of the umbrella's own four clauses and Horizon's object is the branch accounts of the very people its output accused. The rejection: DSM's constitutive power is self-contained in the instrument's own definitional apparatus -- change the criteria and the diagnosed population changes, with nothing outside the instrument needed. Horizon's power over guilt was not self-contained: it required an external legal doctrine (the presumption of proper operation) and prosecutorial conduct (representation of reliability plus non-disclosure), neither of which is part of the tool's spec -- and the Court of Appeal's holding centres exactly those two external elements.
submitted.who_pays: Subpostmasters prosecuted and convicted on Horizon evidence. Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry Vol. 1, para 3.24: "it seems to me to be likely that approximately 1,000 persons were prosecuted and convicted throughout the United Kingdom during the period with which the Inquiry is concerned based on Horizon evidence." The Court of Appeal quashed the convictions of 39 persons on 23 April 2021; the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024 later provided for quashing convictions more widely.
submitted.submission_note: Submitted by the field, not chosen by the collective -- REQUESTS.md, 2026-07-03: the first case in this drawer the collective did not pick for itself.
